 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-58x","title":"fcp.jira: Sprint and board management","description":"# fcp.jira: Sprint and board management (FCP2)

## Goal
Implement Jira Agile operations for **boards** and **sprints** so agents can manage sprint workflows safely under FCP2 capability + approval enforcement.

This bead is specifically about Jira Software (Agile) surfaces (boards, sprints, backlog), not generic issue CRUD.

## Scope
### Read operations (low risk)
- List boards visible to the principal.
- Get board configuration (type, filters, location).
- List sprints for a board; get sprint metadata.
- List issues in a sprint/backlog (pagination; stable ordering).

### Write operations (medium/high risk)
- Create sprint (board-scoped).
- Start / complete sprint.
- Move issues into/out of sprint and backlog.

### Constraints
- Must work in both Jira Cloud and Server/DC (via `flywheel_connectors-3a9`).
- Must respect project permissions and Jira Software license availability.

## Capability model (illustrative)
Capability IDs MUST NOT encode hosts/ports.
- `jira.boards.read`
- `jira.sprints.read`
- `jira.sprints.write` (create/start/complete)
- `jira.sprints.assign_issues` (move issues)

Danger/approval guidance:
- Starting/completing sprints should be **approval-gated** by policy in many orgs.
  - Emit DecisionReceipt reason codes that are specific (e.g., `JIRA_SPRINT_START_REQUIRES_APPROVAL`).

## FCP2 requirements
- Single-zone binding.
- NetworkConstraints pinned to the Jira base hostname.
- For write ops: emit OperationReceipt + AuditEvent with:
  - board_id / sprint_id / issue_keys (IDs only; no secret data)
  - request correlation id

## Deliverables
- Operation schemas:
  - `jira.board.list`
  - `jira.board.get`
  - `jira.sprint.list`
  - `jira.sprint.get`
  - `jira.sprint.create`
  - `jira.sprint.start`
  - `jira.sprint.complete`
  - `jira.sprint.issues`
  - `jira.sprint.move_issues`
  - `jira.backlog.move_issues`
- Robust pagination helpers (bounded memory; streaming-friendly).
- Clear error mapping when Jira Software APIs are unavailable.

## Test strategy (MUST)
### Unit/integration (mock-only)
- Mock fixtures for Agile endpoints:
  - board listing and sprint listing
  - start/complete sprint happy paths
  - permission denied
  - missing Jira Software (feature not enabled)
  - rate-limit + retry/backoff

### Compliance
- Ensure capability-gating is enforced for each operation.
- Ensure write operations produce receipts/audit events.

## Acceptance criteria
- A mock-only test suite covers both Cloud and Server/DC variants.
- All write operations are capability + (policy-configurable) approval gated.
- No assumptions about “global boards”; all operations are explicitly scoped.

Reject tokens with null/empty/missing constraints. Define a DENY-ALL default when constraints are absent (empty resource list = no operations allowed). WHY: H2 Adversarial found capability ceiling constraint bypass (#9, HIGH): if constraints are None, enforce_resource_constraints() is never called. An attacker-controlled issuer can issue unconstrained capabilities to arbitrary resources. J1 Deontic found this violates the default-deny principle — a null constraint should mean no access, not unlimited access. WHERE: crates/fcp-core/src/capability.rs — CapabilityToken verification path, specifically the constraint enforcement section. Change: if constraints is None, return Err(FcpError::CapabilityDenied) instead of skipping. Also update CapabilityToken construction to require constraints. ACCEPTANCE: (1) Tokens without constraints are rejected at verification time. (2) Token construction API requires constraints parameter (not Optional). (3) Empty constraint set = deny all (not allow all). (4) At least 4 tests: token with valid constraints passes, token with null constraints rejected, token with empty constraints rejected, token with overly-broad constraints still works (constraint narrowing is separate). ESTIMATED EFFORT: 1-2 days. DESIGN NOTE: This is one of the highest-impact-to-effort-ratio changes. Removing one Option<> wrapper eliminates an entire class of privilege escalation. DEPENDS ON: C3.1 (phantom tokens) — because the constraint check happens during verify(), and the phantom type ensures verify() is always called.","created_at":"2026-04-08T03:36:49Z"},{"id":2648,"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-62ai5","author":"jemanuel","text":"CROSS-EPIC DEP: Depends on C1.3 (RevocationFreshnessClass in manifests) because both C1.3 and C3.4 add mandatory fields to the capability/manifest validation path. They should be coordinated to avoid two separate breaking changes to the validation pipeline. Implement them together in the same verification method.","created_at":"2026-04-08T03:37:41Z"},{"id":2663,"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-62ai5","author":"jemanuel","text":"C3.4 mandatory constraints: CapabilityConstraints::is_empty(), verification rejects null/empty, constraints_cbor() on CwtClaims+Builder, 4 acceptance tests. Commit 45ecad49.","created_at":"2026-04-10T23:50:45Z"}]}
 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-63o","title":"fcp.zendesk: Ticket CRUD Operations","description":"# fcp.zendesk: Ticket CRUD Operations (FCP2)

## Goal
Provide safe, typed ticket CRUD so agents can:
- create tickets
- read ticket details and thread metadata
- update status/priority/assignee/tags

Ticket content often contains PII; strict logging hygiene is required.

## Scope
- Create/get/update ticket.
- List/search tickets with filters and pagination.
- Soft-delete/archive (dangerous; optional).
- Attachments are handled as bounded downloads/uploads (may be separate surface).

## Capability model (illustrative)
- `zendesk.tickets.read`
- `zendesk.tickets.write` (dangerous-ish)
- Optional: `zendesk.tickets.delete` (dangerous)

## FCP2 requirements
- Single-zone binding.
- NetworkConstraints pinned to the Zendesk subdomain host.
- PII hygiene:
  - do not log raw ticket bodies or requester emails
  - logs include only ticket ids and field names changed
- For writes: receipts + audit events required.

## Deliverables
- Operation schemas:
  - `zendesk.ticket.create`
  - `zendesk.ticket.get`
  - `zendesk.ticket.update`
  - `zendesk.ticket.search`
  - Optional: `zendesk.ticket.archive`

## Test strategy (MUST)
- Mock-only tests for:
  - CRUD flows
  - pagination
  - permission denied
  - receipts/audit for mutations
  - redaction

## Acceptance criteria
- Ticket mutations are mechanically authorized and auditable.
- No PII leaks into logs.

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-674lv","title":"TEST-FUZZ-X: verify_cookie / compute_cookie field-binding metamorphic fuzz","description":"fcp_protocol::session::compute_cookie + verify_cookie (session.rs:989-1026)
implement the stateless DoS-prevention cookie under HMAC-SHA256(cookie_key,
canonical CBOR of from || to || eph_pubkey || nonce || timestamp || suites
|| transport_limits). The host issues this cookie in response to an
unauthenticated hello and refuses to advance the handshake until the peer
echoes a valid one — so the cookie is the gate that closes the trivial
unauthenticated DoS surface against handshake state allocation.

Existing fuzz coverage:
  - decode_cookie_bytes (wire-format length + identity check) — fuzz_session
  - session.rs handshake decode — fuzz_session
  - session_metamorphic — verify_session_mac binding (mac_key, session_id,
    direction, seq, frame), NOT cookie

The metamorphic relations a regression would silently break are NOT
covered:

  1. **Round-trip**: verify_cookie(key, hello, compute_cookie(key, hello))
     MUST return Ok (otherwise the cookie gate is unusable).
  2. **Determinism**: compute_cookie is a pure function — same (key, hello)
     MUST yield byte-identical cookies.
  3. **Field-binding**: bit-flipping any of (from, to, eph_pubkey, nonce,
     timestamp, suites, transport_limits) MUST cause verify_cookie to
     return InvalidCookie. A regression that dropped, e.g., the timestamp
     from compute_cookie's input would let an attacker reuse a captured
     cookie indefinitely; dropping eph_pubkey would let them retrofit a
     fresh ephemeral pubkey to a captured cookie (mid-handshake
     swap-in surface).
  4. **Key-binding**: different cookie_key on identical hello MUST yield
     a cookie that fails verification under the original key.
  5. **Cookie-tamper rejection**: bit-flipping the cookie bytes
     themselves MUST cause InvalidCookie (anchors the ct_eq path at
     session.rs:1021).

Acceptance:
  - fuzz/fuzz_targets/session_cookie_binding.rs
  - 5 MRs above + once-gated regression anchor exercising the timestamp-
    binding path with a hand-constructed pair that defeats the
    "captured cookie reused with a different timestamp" attack.
  - Registered in fuzz/Cargo.toml.
  - cargo +nightly check + clippy -D warnings clean.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"test","assignee":"SunnyMoose","created_at":"2026-04-27T21:30:34.398168Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-27T21:32:26.415703Z","closed_at":"2026-04-27T21:32:26.415221Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"comments":[{"id":3044,"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-674lv","author":"jemanuel","text":"Shipped in commit d25b6659. fuzz/fuzz_targets/session_cookie_binding.rs — 5 MRs (round-trip, determinism, per-field binding ×7, key-binding, cookie-tamper rejection) + once-gated anchors for timestamp-binding (defeats indefinite replay) and eph_pubkey-binding (defeats mid-handshake ephemeral swap-in). cargo +nightly check + clippy -D warnings clean. NOTE: pivoted from session_finish (no such frame exists in fcp-protocol) to verify_cookie — same handshake-DoS-gate area, fuzz-uncovered.","created_at":"2026-04-27T21:32:26Z"}]}

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-k4ka","title":"fcp-graphql: GraphQL Client Infrastructure Library","description":"# fcp-graphql: GraphQL Client Infrastructure Library


## Goal
Provide a shared, typed GraphQL client library for connectors with retries/pagination/subscriptions, mapping errors to the FCP taxonomy and fully mock-tested.

## Overview
Shared GraphQL client library for connectors that interface with GraphQL APIs (Linear, GitHub, Notion, Shopify, etc.).

## Features

### Core Client
- Async GraphQL client with connection pooling
- Query/mutation/subscription support
- Automatic retry with exponential backoff
- Request batching and deduplication
- Query complexity estimation

### Query Building
- Type-safe query builder
- Fragment composition
- Variable handling
- Directive support

### Pagination
- Cursor-based pagination (Relay-style)
- Offset-based pagination
- Automatic page traversal
- Total count estimation

### Subscriptions
- WebSocket transport
- Reconnection handling
- Subscription multiplexing
- Heartbeat management

### Introspection
- Schema introspection caching
- Type validation
- Deprecation warnings

### Error Handling
- GraphQL error parsing
- Partial data handling
- Network error recovery
- Rate limit detection

## Dependencies
- reqwest (HTTP)
- tokio-tungstenite (WebSocket)
- serde_json
- fcp-core

## Consumers
- fcp.linear (GraphQL-only API)
- fcp.github (GraphQL API v4)
- fcp.notion (REST+GraphQL hybrid)
- fcp.shopify (Admin API)

## Testing
- Mock GraphQL server
- Response recording/playback
- Schema validation tests

## Acceptance Criteria
- The functionality described in this bead is implemented with typed schemas + validation (no unstructured “stringly” interfaces).
- Errors map to the FCP error taxonomy with correct retry semantics and no secret/PII leakage.
- Mock-only unit/integration tests cover success paths and common failures and emit structured JSON logs per `flywheel_connectors-1n78.35`.

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-k57","title":"fcp.jira: Bidirectional Beads sync integration","description":"# fcp.jira: Bidirectional Beads sync integration (FCP2)

## Goal
Implement **bidirectional synchronization** between Flywheel Beads issues and Jira issues for the connector’s bound zone.

This enables:
- Jira → Beads: convert incoming Jira work into native Beads (agent-ready tasks)
- Beads → Jira: propagate Beads updates back into Jira to keep humans in their existing workflows

## Non-goals
- “Backwards compatibility” with any older sync format or FCP1 behavior.
- Cross-zone sync from a single connector instance (still single-zone bound).

## Core requirements (hard)
- **Deterministic mapping** (same inputs produce same object updates).
- **Loop prevention** (Jira update triggers Beads update triggers Jira update… must stop).
- **Conflict detection** (concurrent edits) with explicit policy:
  - default: fail-closed + raise a conflict event
  - optional: last-write-wins only if explicitly enabled
- **Idempotency**: repeated webhook deliveries or retries must not duplicate issues/comments.

## Scope
### Mapping surface (minimum viable)
- Bead fields ↔ Jira fields:
  - title ↔ summary
  - description ↔ description (ADF/markdown conversion)
  - status ↔ workflow status
  - priority ↔ priority mapping
  - labels ↔ labels
  - assignee ↔ assignee
  - due date ↔ duedate
  - estimates ↔ timeoriginalestimate (if policy allows)
  - bead_id ↔ a dedicated custom field (or label) for stable correlation

### Sync directions
- `jira_to_beads`
- `beads_to_jira`
- `bidirectional`

### Event sources
- Jira webhooks (issue created/updated/commented/transitioned).
- Beads change events (local event stream from the platform).

## Connector state model (FCP2)
This bead MUST use the platform connector-state mechanism:
- Store a persistent mapping table:
  - `bead_id ↔ jira_issue_id/key`
  - last_seen_revision / fingerprint
  - last_sync_origin (jira|beads)
  - tombstones for deletions/archives
- Use singleton-writer lease semantics if the connector runs in multiple locations.

## Capability + approval model
Illustrative capabilities:
- `jira.issues.read` / `jira.issues.write`
- `jira.comments.write`
- `jira.webhooks.manage` (dangerous; provisioning only)

Policy guidance:
- Creating/transitioning issues in Jira is a write and must produce receipts/audit.
- Bulk sync operations may require an ApprovalToken depending on org policy.

## Deliverables
- A sync engine with:
  - field mapping layer
  - ADF ↔ markdown conversion
  - conflict detection + resolution policy
  - loop prevention via origin stamps + revision fingerprints
- Operation schemas:
  - `jira.sync.push_bead`
  - `jira.sync.pull_issue`
  - `jira.sync.reconcile`
- Structured logging:
  - correlation_id per sync run
  - per-item reason codes for skips/conflicts

## Test strategy (MUST)
### Unit/integration (mock-only)
- Webhook replay tests:
  - duplicate webhook deliveries do not duplicate Beads/Jira updates
- Conflict tests:
  - concurrent edits produce conflict events and do not silently overwrite
- Loop tests:
  - Beads update → Jira update → webhook does not re-trigger Beads update
- State tests:
  - mapping table persists across restarts
  - lease enforcement prevents double writers

### E2E compliance
- Use the shared compliance runner to confirm:
  - default deny on all sync operations
  - receipts/audit for all writes
  - NetworkConstraints enforcement

## Acceptance criteria
- A round-trip sync of a single issue converges in ≤2 sync cycles without loops.
- Conflict cases are explicit, logged, and surfaced as events.
- Sync behavior is deterministic and idempotent.

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-qgu6t","title":"[CRITICAL] Fix non-production default URLs in DocuSign and Plaid connectors","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-25T03:05:12.695374Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-03-25T03:46:53.020397Z","closed_at":"2026-03-25T03:46:53.020226Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["connectors","critical","hardening"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-qgu6t","depends_on_id":"flywheel_connectors-1z643","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-25T03:05:19.245594Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":1938,"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-qgu6t","author":"jemanuel","text":"CRITICAL: Non-Production Default URLs

Two connectors ship DEFAULT_BASE_URL constants pointing at non-production environments. Any deployment using the out-of-the-box defaults will silently hit sandbox/demo infrastructure rather than the provider's production API. This is the most dangerous class of hard-coded constant because it fails silently — requests succeed but against the wrong data.

FINDINGS:

1. DocuSign (connectors/docusign/src/client.rs:38):
   DEFAULT_BASE_URL = "https://demo.docusign.net/restapi/v2.1/accounts"
   - This is the DocuSign DEMO environment, not production
   - Production URLs follow the pattern: https://na1.docusign.net/..., https://eu.docusign.net/..., etc.
   - The connector's doctor output (connector.rs:1050) mentions both demo and production patterns, but the *default* is demo
   - Any user who doesn't explicitly override base_url gets demo

2. Plaid (connectors/plaid/src/client.rs:21):
   DEFAULT_BASE_URL = "https://sandbox.plaid.com"
   - This is the Plaid SANDBOX environment, not production or development
   - Plaid has three environments: sandbox, development, production
   - All data in sandbox is fake test data
   - The connector explicitly names environments (connector.rs:18,22,65) but defaults to sandbox

FIX APPROACH:
- Remove the hard-coded default entirely from both connectors
- Make base_url a required configuration field (no default = forces explicit choice)
- Add environment enum (Production/Sandbox/Demo) to the configure() handler
- Update doctor/self_check to warn if running against non-production
- Update manifest.toml to document the required configuration
- Add tests verifying that unconfigured connectors refuse to start rather than silently hitting demo/sandbox

WHY THIS MATTERS:
FCP's security model is zone-based with capability tokens. A connector that silently routes traffic to a demo server undermines the entire trust chain — real capability tokens are sent to a test environment, potentially leaking production intent to sandbox infrastructure. This is a category of bug that the FCP security invariants should mechanically prevent.","created_at":"2026-03-25T03:05:36Z"}]}
